turns.content: Why does this even work this way? I'm trying to port our existing project that uses OpenSSL for parsing certificate chains and extracting Subject Alternative Names, but the x509-cert crate feels like it's forcing me to manually unwrap every single layer of DER data instead of just giving me a clean string. It seems like we should be able to skip all this manual decoding logic since Rust is supposed to make things easier.

That frustration comes from expecting the high-level API to automatically handle the low-level binary parsing for you, but x509-cert requires you to explicitly call read_der or parse_pem depending on your input format because it needs to know exactly what boundaries exist in the DER structure. You won't get a direct string result without first decoding the bytes into an x509::Certificate object which wraps the parsed ASN.1 data, and then you need to access the raw_der field if you want to manipulate the underlying certificate chain manually.

Okay so I just parse the PEM file into a Certificate struct and then call read_der on it? But wait, isn't that what we already do in our current codebase where we strip the header/footer lines before passing bytes to OpenSSL's d2i_X509 function?

You're making an assumption that removing PEM headers is equivalent to how x509-cert handles input, but it's not quite right because x509-cert expects either raw DER bytes or a string containing the entire PEM block including the -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE----- markers if you use parse_pem. The crate doesn't automatically strip those markers for you like some other libraries might, so you still need to handle the boundary detection yourself before calling read_der on the certificate object.
Ah okay, but what about when we have a full chain? Can x509-cert just validate the entire chain in one go without me having to manually check each individual cert's validity period or trust anchor?

x509-cert can handle full chain validation through its verify_chain method which internally checks the certificate path, but it requires you to provide a list of trusted root certificates beforehand. You need to build an x509::TrustAnchorSet containing all your trusted roots before calling verify_chain on your chain, otherwise the crate won't know which intermediate certs are actually authorized for verification.

Got it, so I need to construct that TrustAnchorSet first. But what if we're extracting SANs and one of our intermediates doesn't have a valid extension? Will x509-cert just skip that cert and continue down the chain, or does it fail immediately?

If an intermediate in your chain lacks a required SAN extension or fails validation checks, x509::Certificate will return an Err variant when you try to access its extensions field. The verify_chain method won't silently skip bad certs; instead it returns VerificationError which tells you exactly which certificate failed the check so you can log that specific error rather than letting your whole application crash.

That makes sense, but I'm worried about performance since we're parsing every single cert individually. Is there a way to batch process multiple certificates at once without iterating through them one by one in our loop?

There isn't a direct batch parser for multiple x509 certs because the crate is designed around individual certificate manipulation rather than bulk operations, but you can optimize your loop by caching parsed Certificate objects and reusing their internal ASN.1 structures if needed. If performance becomes critical, consider checking whether serde_json::Value serialization of parsed certs might help with downstream processing instead of manual byte iteration.
